cards.front: Count from zero to five in Arabic.
cards.back: صفر، واحد، اثنان، ثلاثة، أربعة، خمسة
cards.romanized: sifr, wāḥid, ithnān, thalātha, arbaʿa, khamsa.
cards.grammarNote: These are the cardinal numbers. 1 and 2 agree in gender with the noun they count. 3–10 use the opposite gender — that rule starts with ثلاثة/ثلاث.

cards.front: Count from six to ten in Arabic.
What is the gender reversal rule for Arabic numbers 3–10?
cards.back: Numbers 3–10 use the OPPOSITE gender of the noun they count.
cards.grammarNote: Masculine noun → feminine number (with ة). Feminine noun → masculine number (without ة). Example: ثلاثة كتب (three books, masc. noun → fem. number) vs. ثلاث بنات (three girls, fem. noun → masc. number).
كتاب (kitāb = book) is masculine, so the number takes the feminine form ثلاثة (with ة). The counted noun appears in the plural: كتب (kutub).
بنت (bint = girl) is feminine, so the number takes the masculine form ثلاث (without ة). The counted noun is plural: بنات (banāt).
أصدقاء (aṣdiqāʾ = friends) is masculine plural, so the number is feminine: خمسة (khamsa, with ة).
Compound numbers above 20 put the unit first: واحد وعشرون = 'one and twenty' = 21. The و (wa = and) connects them.
مئة (miʾa) = 100. ألف (alf) = 1000. To say 200: مئتان (miʾatān, dual form). To say 2000: ألفان (alfān).
Time uses ordinal numbers: الثانية (the second), الثالثة (the third), etc. الساعة (al-sāʿa) = the clock/hour. No verb needed — this is an equational sentence.
النصف (al-niṣf) = the half. الربع (al-rubʿ) = the quarter. So: الثالثة والربع = 3:15 (three and a quarter).
تذاكر (tadhākir = tickets) is feminine, so the number is masculine: ثلاث (thalāth, without ة).
